original_abstract:
This paper introduces the Deep Gradient Network (DGNet), a high-performance framework designed for efficient camouflaged object detection (COD). Unlike conventional methods that rely on sparse boundary cues or global masks, DGNet leverages object-level gradient supervision to exploit discriminative intensity changes within camouflaged regions. The architecture bifurcates the learning process into two specialized streams: a context encoder for high-level semantic reasoning and a texture encoder for structural detail extraction. To integrate these distinct representations, we propose a Gradient-Induced Transition (GIT) module, which employs a soft grouping strategy to adaptively fuse multi-source features across parallel sub-spaces. 

Experimental results across three primary COD benchmarks demonstrate that DGNet sets a new state-of-the-art performance standard. To address practical deployment needs, we also present DGNet-S, a lightweight variant that achieves real-time inference (80 fps). Despite using only 6.82% of the parameters of recent top-tier models, DGNet-S maintains competitive accuracy. Furthermore, we demonstrate the robustness and versatility of our framework through its successful application to downstream tasks, including polyp segmentation in medical imaging, industrial defect detection, and transparent object segmentation. Our code and models are publicly available.
humanized_abstract:
Object camouflaging phenomena prevail in various visual scenes and have drawn increasing attention in computer vision. In contrast to classical object detection problems, camouflaged objects present unique challenges, such as non-learnable boundaries and unclear distinctions with surrounding contexts. To this end, this paper proposes a Deep Gradient Network, namely DGNet, aiming to facilitate camouflaged object detection (COD) with enhanced performance. To the best of our knowledge, DGNet is the first attempt to investigate the COD task by employing gradient information for guiding object detection. In contrast to models that heavily depend on sparse boundary contours or complete instance masks, we devise DGNet to utilize object gradient, which explores discriminative intensity changes within camouflaged objects. Benefiting from the gradient information, our DGNet adopts a two-branch structure called context encoder and texture encoder to facilitate semantic understanding of surrounding contexts and fine-grained object textures, respectively. In order to properly combine the complementary information in the two branches, we introduce a Gradient-Induced Transition (GIT) module, which utilizes soft grouping to achieve adaptive multi-source feature fusion in different sub-spaces.

Experiments on three primary COD benchmarks show that DGNet achieves state-of-the-art performance. To fulfill the needs of the practice, we also introduce a real-time branch, i.e., DGNet-S. A lightweight version of DGNet, it is capable of achieving real-time inference speed of 80 fps with parameters being only 6.82% of those used in recent winning models. In addition, our model shows great adaptability for the downstream tasks, and it achieves notable performance for polyp segmentation of endoscopy images, industrial defect detection and transparent object segmentation. Codes and models are open to public.

Experiments on three primary COD benchmarks show that DGNet achieves state-of-the-art performance. To fulfill the needs of the practice, we also introduce a real-time branch, i.e., DGNet-S. document.input:
This paper introduces the Deep Gradient Network (DGNet), a high-performance framework designed for efficient camouflaged object detection (COD). Experimental results across three primary COD benchmarks demonstrate that DGNet sets a new state-of-the-art performance standard.
